abstract:
Since its emergence in 2006, the development of bifunctional organocatalysts incorporating both a thiourea moiety and a primary amine group has significantly expanded the toolkit of asymmetric synthesis. These versatile catalysts operate through a dual-activation mechanism, where the primary amine typically forms covalent intermediates—such as enamines or iminium ions—while the thiourea unit coordinates and activates electrophiles via hydrogen bonding. This synergistic approach mimics the catalytic strategies found in natural enzymes like type I aldolases. Over the past several years, various structural frameworks including diamines, saccharides, and cinchona alkaloid derivatives have been utilized to design highly efficient primary amine-thiourea catalysts.

This review systematically summarizes the recent progress in the application of these catalysts across a wide range of enantioselective transformations. Key reactions discussed include nitro-Michael additions, conjugate additions to unsaturated carbonyl compounds, Mannich-type reactions, and α-alkylations. Furthermore, the utility of these catalysts in complex cascade processes and cycloadditions, such as the Nazarov cyclization, Diels–Alder, and Biginelli reactions, is highlighted. By examining mechanistic studies and computational models, this perspective illustrates how the unique interplay of functional groups enables high levels of stereocontrol and reactivity in the synthesis of diverse chiral building blocks and bioactive molecules.
